%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 2.3 The Unit Impulse Response h(t)

> **Section Objective:** Learn what the unit impulse response h(t) is and why it is the key response used to predict LTIC system behavior.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- unit impulse response h(t)
- impulse decomposition of an input
- zero initial conditions at t = 0^-
- LTIC differential-equation operator form
- M ≤ N and the general case M = N

## 1. Why h(t) Matters

Any input \(x(t)\) can be mentally broken into many very narrow rectangular pulses. As the pulse width shrinks toward zero, each piece behaves like a scaled impulse component. Because an LTIC system obeys superposition, the total output is simply the sum of the responses to all those impulse pieces.

The core payoff: **if we know the response to one unit impulse, \(h(t)\), we can build the response to any more complicated input.**

A short burst at time \(\tau\) creates a shifted copy of the impulse response, starting at \(\tau\).

### EXAM TIP

When a problem asks for the output from an arbitrary input in an LTIC system, \(h(t)\) is the response pattern you look for first.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Definition of the Unit Impulse Response

The input \(\delta(t)\) is a unit impulse — it acts only at the single instant \(t=0\) and is zero everywhere else. The output \(h(t)\) is what the system produces in response to that input.

**Zero initial conditions at \(t=0^-\)** means the system is completely unexcited just before the impulse arrives — no stored energy, no prior activity.

Think of a lightning strike: the bolt lasts an instant, but it can charge a capacitor or excite a resonance that continues long afterward. The impulse vanishes immediately, yet it can create nonzero internal conditions that drive the system response.

**Minimal example:** If \(h(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)\), then after \(t=0\) the impulse is gone, but the output decays as \(e^{-2t}\) for all \(t > 0\).

#### COMMON MISUSE

Do not say \(h(t)\) is the impulse itself. \(\delta(t)\) is the input; \(h(t)\) is the response to that input.

$$h(t) = \text{response to } \delta(t) \text{ applied at } t=0 \text{ with zero initial conditions at } t=0^-$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. The LTIC Equation Behind h(t)

Eq. (2.11) is the compact **operator form** of the \(N\)th-order constant-coefficient differential equation describing the LTIC system.

**The operator \(D\)** means differentiate with respect to time:

$$D\,y(t) = \frac{dy(t)}{dt}, \quad D^N y(t) = \frac{d^N y(t)}{dt^N}$$

- **Left side:** collects all derivatives of the output \(y(t)\).
- **Right side:** collects all derivatives of the input \(x(t)\).

**When to use this form:** whenever the system is described by a constant-coefficient ODE and you need to find or reason about the impulse response.

**Practical constraint:** noise considerations restrict real systems to \(M \le N\), where \(M\) is the degree of the right-side polynomial and \(N\) is the degree of the left-side polynomial. The most general practical case discussed here is \(M = N\).

#### COMMON MISUSE

Do not treat \(D\) as an ordinary algebraic number in general arithmetic. It is the differentiation operator. Operator algebra rules apply.

$$\left(D^N + a_1 D^{N-1} + \cdots + a_{N-1}D + a_N\right)y(t) = \left(b_0 D^N + b_1 D^{N-1} + \cdots + b_{N-1}D + b_N\right)x(t) \tag{2.11}$$

### WORKED READING EXAMPLE

Consider the system equation:

$$\left(D + 3\right)y(t) = 2\,x(t)$$

This is **first order** because the highest output derivative is \(D^1 y(t) = dy(t)/dt\).

To find the impulse response, set \(x(t) = \delta(t)\) and enforce zero initial conditions at \(t = 0^-\). The output \(y(t)\) then becomes \(h(t)\) by definition.

No further solving is needed here — the goal is to recognize the setup.

> **Quick check:** If the input symbol becomes \(\delta(t)\), the output symbol becomes \(h(t)\).

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(h(t)\) = response to \(\delta(t)\) with zero initial conditions at \(t=0^-\); the impulse is the cause, \(h(t)\) is the effect.
- Eq. (2.11): \(\left(D^N+a_1D^{N-1}+\cdots+a_N\right)y(t)=\left(b_0D^N+\cdots+b_N\right)x(t)\) — operator form of the LTIC ODE.
- \(D^k y(t) = d^k y(t)/dt^k\); \(D\) is the differentiation operator, not a plain number.
- Practical constraint: \(M \le N\); the general case treated here is \(M = N\).

*Next, this impulse-response idea becomes a practical method for computing system outputs.*
